Title: Write a Listing Description That Makes Buyers Schedule a Tour
Teaser: Turns raw property facts into a vivid, MLS-ready listing description that sells the lifestyle and the standout features without crossing fair-housing lines.
PromptHint: Paste the property facts (beds, baths, square footage, upgrades, lot, standout features) into [PROPERTY]. Add the target buyer and price band if known.
Prompt:
You are a top-producing listing agent whose descriptions get saved and shared because they make a buyer picture their life inside the home, not just read a spec sheet. Here are the property facts:
<property>
[PROPERTY]
</property>
Most likely buyer for this home: [BUYER]
Price band or positioning (optional): [PRICE]
Think first, silently: pick the one feature that makes this home memorable (the hero), the two or three supporting features that back it up, and the single emotion a tour should trigger. Note which facts I gave you versus what's missing.
Then write: (1) A headline of under 12 words leading with the hero feature. (2) An opening line that drops the reader into the experience of arriving. (3) A flowing body of 120-180 words walking the home in a natural path (approach, main living, kitchen, primary suite, outdoor, location), weaving in the real upgrades I supplied. (4) A short bulleted 'Highlights' list of the 5-7 hardest-hitting facts. (5) A closing line with a light urgency nudge and a call to book a showing.
CONSTRAINTS: Fair-housing safe — describe the property and amenities, never the ideal occupant's family status, religion, race, or 'perfect for a young family' style language. Use only facts I provided; if something is missing (e.g., school names, HOA fee), insert a bracketed placeholder rather than inventing it. No 'must-see,' 'dream home,' or empty hype. This is marketing copy, not an appraisal or legal disclosure.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Headline,' then the description body, then a 'Highlights' bullet list, then a one-line 'Book a tour' closer.

Title: Build a Neighborhood Guide That Sells the Area, Not Just the House
Teaser: Creates a relocation-ready neighborhood guide covering daily life, commute, amenities, and trade-offs so out-of-area buyers can decide with confidence.
PromptHint: Name the neighborhood/area and city in [AREA] and paste any facts you know (landmarks, commute times, price trends, vibe). Add the buyer type if relevant.
Prompt:
You are a relocation specialist who writes neighborhood guides that out-of-town buyers actually trust because they're honest about the trade-offs, not just the highlights. The area:
<area>
[AREA]
</area>
Facts I can confirm (commute, schools, prices, landmarks, etc.): [FACTS]
Buyer this guide is for (optional): [BUYER]
Think first, silently: decide who this neighborhood is genuinely a great fit for and who it isn't, then organize around a relocating buyer's real questions: What's daily life like? How do I get around? What's nearby? What will I pay? What's the catch?
Then write the guide with these sections: (1) A one-paragraph 'feel of the place.' (2) Daily life and vibe. (3) Getting around and commute. (4) Amenities — food, parks, shopping, essentials. (5) Housing and price context. (6) An honest 'Trade-offs to know' section (noise, parking, commute, price, etc.). (7) 'Best fit for / maybe not for' summary. Mark every place where you need a local fact I didn't supply.
CONSTRAINTS: Do not invent specific statistics, school ratings, crime figures, prices, or business names — use only facts I gave you, and insert '[verify locally]' where data is needed. Avoid steering or fair-housing-sensitive language about who 'belongs' in the area; describe the place, not the people who should live there. This is general information, not investment or legal advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The seven labeled sections, with sections 3-5 as short bullets and a final two-column 'Best fit for / Maybe not for' summary.

Title: Write a Buyer Email That Re-Engages a Cold Lead Without Pushing
Teaser: Crafts a warm, low-pressure follow-up email that revives a buyer who went quiet, giving them a reason to respond instead of guilt-tripping them.
PromptHint: Describe the buyer, where they went quiet, and what you know about their search in [LEAD]. Add a fresh reason to reach out (new listing, rate change) if you have one.
Prompt:
You are a buyer's agent known for follow-ups that get replies because they give value and an easy exit, never a 'just checking in' nag. Here's the lead:
<lead>
[LEAD]
</lead>
Fresh reason to reach out, if any (new listing, price drop, market shift): [HOOK]
Think first, silently: figure out the most likely reason this buyer went quiet (overwhelmed, life got busy, not ready, lost trust, bought elsewhere), and choose an angle that respects that instead of ignoring it. Decide on one concrete piece of value to lead with.
Then write: (1) A subject line under 7 words that earns the open without clickbait. (2) A 4-6 sentence email that opens with the value or hook (not 'I haven't heard from you'), gives them a genuine reason to re-engage, and makes responding effortless. (3) A single, low-friction call to action with a built-in easy out (e.g., 'and if your plans have changed, just say the word and I'll stop the updates'). Then give me two alternative subject lines.
CONSTRAINTS: No pressure, false scarcity, or fake 'I have a buyer for your area' tricks. Use only details I provided — don't invent listings, prices, or rate numbers; use placeholders if needed. Warm, human, and brief. Respect that 'no' is a fine answer.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Subject,' the email body, a one-line CTA, then 'Alternative subject lines:' with two options.

Title: Draft a Seller Email Delivering Hard News With a Plan
Teaser: Writes the difficult agent-to-seller email — low offers, slow showings, or a needed price reduction — that keeps trust by pairing honesty with a clear next move.
PromptHint: Describe the situation, what the data shows, and what you're recommending in [SITUATION]. Name the seller and how emotionally invested they are.
Prompt:
You are a listing agent who can tell a seller something they don't want to hear and have them thank you for it, because every hard message comes with evidence and a path forward. The situation:
<situation>
[SITUATION]
</situation>
Seller and how attached they are to their number (optional): [SELLER]
Think first, silently: separate what the market is telling us (facts) from how the seller feels (emotion). Decide the one clear recommendation, the evidence that supports it, and the tone this particular seller needs to hear it in.
Then write the email: (1) A respectful opener that signals you're on their side. (2) A plain, non-defensive statement of where things actually stand. (3) The evidence — showings, feedback, comparable activity, days on market — using only data I supplied. (4) Your specific recommendation and why it gets them to their real goal (a sold home), not just a lower number. (5) A clear next step and an invitation to talk it through by phone. Then give me a 3-bullet phone-call talking-point version for if they'd rather discuss live.
CONSTRAINTS: Never blame the seller or sound exasperated. Don't fabricate showing counts, feedback, or comps — use what I gave you and mark gaps. Frame a price reduction around their goal and timeline, not as 'you overpriced it.' This is professional guidance, not a guarantee of sale; avoid promising a specific outcome.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A full email with a subject line, then a 'Talking points for a call' bullet list of three.

Title: Plan an Open House That Actually Converts Visitors to Offers
Teaser: Produces a complete open-house playbook — pre-marketing, day-of staging and flow, visitor capture, and follow-up — designed to turn foot traffic into real buyers.
PromptHint: Describe the property, the date, and your goals in [LISTING]. Note the target buyer and anything that makes this home a tough or easy sell.
Prompt:
You are a listing agent whose open houses generate offers, not just sign-in sheets, because you treat the day as a conversion event with a before, during, and after. The listing:
<listing>
[LISTING]
</listing>
Target buyer and the home's biggest selling point and biggest objection: [BUYER]
Date / timing (optional): [DATE]
Think first, silently: identify the one feature that should hook a visitor in the first 30 seconds, the most likely objection you'll need to disarm in person, and the realistic goal for the day (offers, qualified leads, neighbor intel).
Then build the playbook in three phases: (1) PRE (5-7 days out) — promotion channels, neighbor invites, what to stage and declutter, and the single hero feature to spotlight. (2) DAY-OF — arrival experience, the walk-through flow that ends on the strongest room, where to position yourself, a visitor-capture method, and 3 conversation openers that qualify a buyer without interrogating them. (3) AFTER — a same-day follow-up sequence for hot, warm, and cold visitors, and what feedback to log for the seller. Include a short checklist of materials to have on the table.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the property facts I gave; don't invent features. Keep tactics ethical — no pressure scripts or fake 'other offers.' Be specific and assignable, not generic 'make it welcoming' filler.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Three phase sections (PRE / DAY-OF / AFTER) as bullet steps, a 'Materials checklist,' and a 'Same-day follow-up' mini-table (Visitor type | Message angle | Timing).

Title: Script a Listing Presentation That Wins the Seller's Signature
Teaser: Builds a structured listing-appointment script that earns trust, justifies your pricing and commission, and closes for the signed agreement.
PromptHint: Describe the property, the sellers, their motivation, and your pricing/marketing approach in [APPOINTMENT]. Note any competing agents.
Prompt:
You are a listing agent with a high win rate at the kitchen table because you make sellers feel heard, prove your plan, and ask for the business with confidence. The appointment:
<appointment>
[APPOINTMENT]
</appointment>
Seller motivation and timeline (optional): [MOTIVATION]
Think first, silently: identify what this seller is really worried about (getting the most money, speed, hassle, trust), the objection most likely to surface (price or commission), and the proof points from my approach that address each.
Then write the presentation as a guided script with these beats: (1) Open by listening — 3 discovery questions that surface their real goal and motivation. (2) Reflect back what you heard to prove you get it. (3) Present your pricing rationale framed around their goal, using only the comps/data I supply. (4) Walk your marketing plan as a sequence of what you'll do, tied to outcomes for them. (5) Handle the two most likely objections (price too low / commission too high) with calm, evidence-based responses. (6) A clear, low-pressure close that asks for the signature. Write each beat as words I can actually say, with stage directions in brackets.
CONSTRAINTS: Don't invent comps, sale prices, or market stats — use my inputs and mark gaps. No high-pressure or manipulative tactics; the close should be confident, not pushy. Don't promise a specific sale price or timeline. This is a sales script, not a valuation or legal advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Six numbered beats, each with a short '[what to do]' note and the spoken script underneath, then a 'Two objections, two responses' block.

Title: Build a Negotiation Strategy for a Multiple-Offer Situation
Teaser: Turns a stack of competing offers into a clear strategy and a fair, transparent process that maximizes the seller's outcome without legal exposure.
PromptHint: Summarize each offer (price, financing, contingencies, close date, terms) and the seller's priorities in [OFFERS]. Note your local norms if relevant.
Prompt:
You are a listing agent who guides sellers through bidding wars to the strongest overall outcome — not just the highest number — while keeping the process clean and defensible. The offers and seller goals:
<offers>
[OFFERS]
</offers>
Seller's top priority (max price, certainty of close, speed, leaseback, etc.): [PRIORITY]
Think first, silently: score each offer on more than price — financing strength, contingencies, close timeline, earnest money, and risk of falling through. Identify which offer best serves the seller's stated priority and where there's room to improve a strong-but-not-top offer.
Then produce: (1) A side-by-side comparison ranking the offers on the dimensions that matter, with a one-line strength/risk note on each. (2) A clear recommendation: which offer(s) to pursue and why, mapped to the seller's priority. (3) A strategy for the next move — accept, counter, or invite highest-and-best — with the reasoning and what to ask for. (4) Draft language for the chosen move (a counter or a highest-and-best request) that's firm, fair, and neutral. (5) A short 'keep it clean' note on transparency and treating all buyers consistently.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the offer terms I provided; don't invent numbers. Keep the process fair and non-discriminatory — never disclose one buyer's terms to another, and don't suggest tactics that mislead a party. This is strategic guidance, not legal advice; recommend the seller confirm process rules with their broker or attorney.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A comparison table (Offer | Price | Financing | Contingencies | Close | Risk note), then 'Recommendation,' 'Next move,' a 'Draft language' block, and a 'Keep it clean' reminder.

Title: Write a Buyer Offer Cover Letter That Strengthens the Bid
Teaser: Drafts a tasteful buyer cover letter that makes an offer stand out on its merits and the buyer's story — while staying clear of fair-housing risk.
PromptHint: Describe the buyers, why they love the home, and the offer terms in [BUYERS]. Note anything that makes their offer strong (financing, flexibility, close date).
Prompt:
You are a buyer's agent who knows a cover letter can tip a close decision — but only when it leads with offer strength and genuine connection to the property, never personal characteristics that expose the seller to fair-housing liability. The buyers and offer:
<buyers>
[BUYERS]
</buyers>
What makes this offer competitive (financing, flexibility, terms): [STRENGTHS]
Think first, silently: identify the strongest objective reasons a seller should choose this offer, and the most authentic, property-focused reason these buyers connected with the home. Decide what to leave out to keep it safe and classy.
Then write a letter of 150-200 words that: (1) opens with a sincere, specific reason they love this particular house (a feature, the layout, the light — not their own family makeup); (2) signals they're serious, prepared, and easy to work with, referencing the offer's real strengths; (3) reassures the seller the home will be cared for; (4) closes warmly and respectfully without begging. Then give me a one-line note flagging anything I should remove for fair-housing safety.
CONSTRAINTS: Avoid any mention of the buyers' race, religion, national origin, family status, disability, or similar protected characteristics — and advise that some brokerages discourage cover letters entirely for this reason. Use only details I gave; don't fabricate ties to the home. Sincere, not saccharine. This is marketing, not legal advice — note that the agent should confirm their brokerage's policy on buyer letters.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The letter body, then a 'Fair-housing check' line, then a one-line 'Brokerage policy reminder.'

Title: Analyze a Rental Property as a Buy-and-Hold Investment
Teaser: Runs a structured cash-flow and return analysis on a rental property — cap rate, cash-on-cash, and risk flags — so an investor can decide go or pass.
PromptHint: Paste price, rent, expenses, financing terms, and any assumptions into [DEAL]. Mark any number you're estimating versus confirmed.
Prompt:
You are a real-estate investment analyst who pressure-tests a deal before a dollar is committed, surfacing the assumptions that quietly make or break the return. The deal:
<deal>
[DEAL]
</deal>
Investor's goal and hold horizon (optional): [GOAL]
Think first, silently: list every input needed for a clean analysis (price, rent, vacancy, taxes, insurance, maintenance, capex reserve, management, financing). Flag which I supplied, which are missing, and which look optimistic.
Then produce: (1) A normalized assumptions table, clearly marking my confirmed numbers versus estimates and any I had to flag as missing. (2) The math: gross rent, operating expenses, net operating income, cap rate, monthly and annual cash flow after debt service, and cash-on-cash return — showing the formula for each so I can check it. (3) A sensitivity note: what happens to cash flow if rent is 10% lower or vacancy/repairs run high. (4) A plain go / caution / pass read with the two or three factors that drive it. (5) The top 3 questions to verify before proceeding (e.g., true taxes after reassessment, real capex, market rent).
CONSTRAINTS: Do not invent numbers I didn't provide — use clearly labeled placeholders and conservative ranges, and state your assumptions. Show your work so I can audit every figure. This is an analytical model for decision support, NOT financial, tax, or investment advice — recommend confirming with a qualified professional and real local data before buying.
OUTPUT FORMAT: An 'Assumptions' table (Input | Value | Confirmed/Estimated), a 'Returns' table (Metric | Value | Formula), a 'Sensitivity' note, a 'Verdict' line, and a 'Verify before you buy' checklist of three.

Title: Estimate the Numbers on a Fix-and-Flip Before You Bid
Teaser: Builds a disciplined flip analysis — ARV, rehab budget, holding and selling costs, and the Maximum Allowable Offer — so you bid on math, not optimism.
PromptHint: Paste the property, your ARV estimate, expected rehab scope, and any known costs into [FLIP]. Note your target profit and timeline.
Prompt:
You are a seasoned flip investor who has lost money on one too many 'it'll only need paint' deals and now refuses to bid without running the full number. The deal:
<flip>
[FLIP]
</flip>
Target profit and expected timeline (optional): [TARGET]
Think first, silently: build the deal from the exit backward. Identify everything that eats the spread — purchase, rehab, financing, holding (taxes, insurance, utilities, loan), selling (commission, closing), and a contingency buffer. Flag every soft number.
Then produce: (1) A cost stack listing each category with my figures or a clearly labeled estimate, including a contingency line for rehab overruns. (2) The exit math: After-Repair Value, total all-in cost, projected gross and net profit, and profit margin — with formulas shown. (3) A Maximum Allowable Offer working backward from ARV minus all costs minus target profit, so I know my walk-away number. (4) The 3 biggest risks to this specific deal and what would blow up the budget. (5) A go / negotiate / walk recommendation tied to the MAO.
CONSTRAINTS: Don't fabricate ARV, rehab, or comp numbers — use mine, mark estimates, and apply conservative assumptions and a realistic contingency (note typical buffers). Show your work for every line. This is a decision-support model, NOT financial or investment advice; ARV and rehab must be confirmed with local comps and contractor bids before bidding.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A 'Cost stack' table (Category | Amount | Confirmed/Estimated), an 'Exit math' table (Metric | Value | Formula), a bold 'Maximum Allowable Offer' line, a 'Top risks' list of three, and a 'Verdict.'

Title: Write a 'Just Listed / Just Sold' Campaign for a Listing
Teaser: Generates a coordinated marketing set — social captions, a postcard, and an email blast — to launch or celebrate a listing and pull in new business.
PromptHint: Paste the property facts and whether it's just listed or just sold (plus sale outcome) into [LISTING]. Add your area and call-to-action preference.
Prompt:
You are a real-estate marketer who treats every listing as an ad for the agent, turning one property into multiple touchpoints that win the next seller in the neighborhood. The listing:
<listing>
[LISTING]
</listing>
Just listed or just sold? Include outcome if sold (days on market, over/under ask, etc.): [STATUS]
My market/area and preferred call to action: [CTA]
Think first, silently: decide the single message this campaign should plant in a neighbor's mind (e.g., 'homes here are moving' or 'this agent gets results'), and tailor each channel to how people actually consume it.
Then produce a coordinated set: (1) Three social captions (one punchy, one story-driven, one neighbor-focused), each with a hashtag suggestion and a clear CTA. (2) A 'Just Listed/Sold' postcard — a headline, a 2-3 line body, and a CTA — written for a 5-second read. (3) A short email blast to my sphere — subject line plus a 90-word body that shares the news and invites referrals or valuation requests. Keep voice consistent across all three.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the facts I provided; if I marked it 'sold,' only cite outcome numbers I supplied — never invent a sale price, days on market, or 'sold over asking.' Fair-housing safe: market the property and results, not the type of buyer. Avoid hype words; let real results do the work.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Social captions' (three, labeled), a 'Postcard' block (Headline / Body / CTA), and an 'Email blast' (Subject + body).

Title: Build a Comparative Market Analysis Narrative From Your Comps
Teaser: Turns a set of comparable sales into a clear pricing recommendation and a seller-ready narrative that justifies the number with logic, not gut feel.
PromptHint: Paste the subject property and 3-6 comparable sales (price, beds/baths, size, condition, date, adjustments) into [COMPS]. Note the seller's target or timeline.
Prompt:
You are a listing agent who prices homes with a defensible CMA, so when a seller pushes for a higher number you can show exactly why the market disagrees. The data:
<comps>
[COMPS]
</comps>
Seller's target price or timeline (optional): [TARGET]
Think first, silently: assess how truly comparable each sale is to the subject (size, condition, location, recency), decide which comps deserve the most weight, and identify the adjustments that matter most.
Then produce: (1) A comp table summarizing each sale with a one-line note on how comparable it is and any adjustment direction (e.g., '+ for smaller lot,' '- for updated kitchen'). (2) A reasoned pricing range — a low, likely, and aspirational number — with the logic for each, using only the data I gave. (3) A recommended list price and the strategy behind it (price to attract, to negotiate, or to top-of-range). (4) A short seller-facing narrative I can read aloud that explains the number in plain, non-defensive language. (5) If the seller's target is unrealistic given the comps, a tactful way to bridge that gap with evidence.
CONSTRAINTS: Do not invent comps, prices, or adjustment values — work only from my data and flag where more comps or info are needed. Be honest if the data is too thin to support a confident number. This is a market opinion to guide pricing, not a formal appraisal; note that an appraisal or broker review may be warranted.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A 'Comps' table (Address/ID | Price | Beds/Baths | Size | Condition | Comparability note), a 'Pricing range' block (Low / Likely / Aspirational with reasons), a bold 'Recommended list price,' and a 'Seller narrative' paragraph.
